title: Achillea ceretanica
intro: Achillea ceretanica, also known as Sardinian yarrow, is a perennial plant belonging to the sunflower family (Asteraceae) native to the Mediterranean region. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, with potential health benefits including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant effects, which may aid in wound healing, reducing fever, and protecting against oxidative stress. The bioactive constituents of Achillea ceretanica include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and essential oils, which contribute to its therapeutic properties. Traditionally, the plant is used to make herbal teas, infusions, and tinctures, as well as being incorporated into topical creams and ointments for external use.
taxonomy: Achillea ceretanica is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Asteraceae. This plant is native to the island of Ceret, located off the coast of France in the Mediterranean Sea. It is a member of the Achillea genus, which comprises approximately 70 species of plants commonly known as yarrow or sneezewort. The plant's classification is further supported by its characteristics, such as its fern-like foliage and small white or yellow flowers. Through its classification, botanists can better understand the plant's relationships with other species in the Asteraceae family.

side_effects: Achillea ceretanica, also known as Ceretan Yarrow, is a plant with potential herbal uses.
